mainEntity.name: Work Vehicle Accident FAQ: Utah and Idaho Law
mainEntity.text: Work Vehicle Accident FAQ: Utah and Idaho Law

acceptedAnswer.text:
Frequently Asked Questions: Work Vehicle Accidents

Am I covered if I was driving my own car for a work errand?

Yes. If you were performing a task for your employer, such as picking up lunch for a meeting or dropping off mail, you are covered by workers' compensation regardless of car ownership. The 'course of employment' is what determines eligibility, not the vehicle title.

Can I get a settlement for pain and suffering?

Not through workers' compensation directly. Workers' comp only covers medical bills and lost wages. However, if another driver was at fault for the accident, you can file a separate personal injury lawsuit against them to recover non-economic damages like pain and suffering.

What happens if my boss tells me not to file a claim?

It is illegal for an employer to retaliate against you for filing a workers' compensation claim. If your boss is pressuring you to 'use your own health insurance,' they are likely trying to keep their premium costs down at your expense. You should ignore this pressure and protect your legal rights by filing the claim and seeking counsel.

Who pays my medical bills if the other driver was uninsured?

If you were working, your employer's workers' compensation insurance is the primary payer for your medical treatment. If you also have Uninsured Motorist (UM) coverage on your personal policy or through your company’s commercial policy, that can provide additional compensation for losses not covered by workers' comp.
